behaviors.description: Cache keys generated from identical query parameters are always identical, and keys from different parameters never collide.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is the key-derivation logic itself (canonicalization/serialization/hash input), which is a single local rule set. No real OS/network/subprocess is required, and no cross-module state handoff is necessary to expose this behavior’s core failures. Higher levels were rejected because the behavior text does not require DB/cache-process interaction, only deterministic mapping from parameters to keys.
behaviors.test_description: Test the key generator as a pure function with table-driven and property-style inputs: same parameter sets (including reordered equivalent params) must yield identical keys; intentionally different parameter sets must yield different keys across a large representative corpus.

behaviors.description: The TTL for a cache entry is computed correctly based on the data category's configured freshness rule (e.g., user profiles = 5 minutes, product catalog = 1 hour).
behaviors.justification: This is deterministic rule evaluation (category -> TTL) and can fail within one local computation path. Real infrastructure is not needed to reproduce incorrect mapping/math. Higher levels were rejected because the behavior text is about TTL computation correctness, not config loading transport, distributed propagation, or runtime clock/OS effects.
behaviors.test_description: Test the TTL computation function with a fixed config fixture and fixed clock input: assert exact TTL outputs per category and fallback/default behavior for unknown categories.

behaviors.description: When memory usage exceeds the configured threshold, the eviction strategy switches from LRU to LFU as specified in the configuration.
behaviors.justification: Real failure typically appears at module handoff: memory-usage signal + threshold config + strategy selector state transition. This needs in-process wiring of multiple components, not just one pure function. System was rejected because the behavior can be reproduced with an injected/in-process memory metric provider; no real OS memory primitive is strictly required by the text. Agentic/Workflow are disqualified (no AI decision path).
behaviors.test_description: Wire cache manager, config, and a controllable memory-metric provider in-process. Start below threshold (assert LRU), then raise reported usage above threshold and assert strategy flips to LFU and remains consistent across subsequent operations.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration is necessary here because the test exercises cross-module state handoff; a single-function unit test would miss wiring/transition bugs.

behaviors.description: A write to the underlying PostgreSQL table invalidates all cache entries whose keys match the affected rows.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary includes real PostgreSQL write semantics and invalidation propagation timing/contract (e.g., trigger/notification/commit ordering). In-process mocks can hide real integration faults with DB events and transaction boundaries. Lower levels were rejected because the behavior explicitly requires a real write to PostgreSQL causing invalidation side effects.
behaviors.test_description: Run against a real PostgreSQL instance and the real cache invalidation path. Seed cache entries for target rows, perform an actual table write/commit, then assert matching cache keys are invalidated while unrelated keys remain.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: System level is required because the assertion depends on real DB-driven invalidation behavior, not just local function logic.

behaviors.description: The eviction policy removes the correct entries — least-recently-used under LRU, least-frequently-used under LFU — when the cache is full.
behaviors.justification: This is core policy algorithm correctness for deterministic data-structure behavior under controlled access patterns. Real OS/network/subprocess is not needed. Integration was rejected because behavior text focuses on which entry is evicted under each policy, not cross-component switching (covered separately by behavior 3).
behaviors.test_description: For each policy implementation, execute deterministic put/get sequences to establish recency/frequency, fill cache to capacity, add one more item, and assert the exact expected victim key is evicted.
